ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 represents chapati/roti as a flatbread food product; tawa roti is a specific griddle-cooked variant. Closest parent used as no exact tawa roti code exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chapatti/roti as a category; tawa roti is a griddle-cooked preparation variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for tawa roti or chapati flatbread as a food item in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium tawa roti piece weighing approximately 60 g, equivalent to one standard serving as commonly consumed in Indian households and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati / Roti (without fat) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports chapati/roti made from whole wheat atta cooked on a tawa without added fat. Values are per 100 g edible portion. This is the primary approved source for Indian dishes and provides a direct match for plain tawa roti.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chapati / whole wheat roti — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283) for whole wheat chapati / roti cooked on a griddle without fat. Tawa roti is functionally identical to plain chapati in composition and cooking method; GI 52 (low) is a defensible direct analogue. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 61.0 × 0.60) / 100 = 19.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~120 mg per 100 g); low saturated fat; high potassium relative to sodium
hypertension.guidance: Plain tawa roti contains moderate sodium (~72 mg per piece) and no added salt beyond preparation; individuals with hypertension may include it as part of a low-sodium meal plan, but should monitor total sodium from accompaniments such as curries and chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (61 g per 100 g) offset by low GI (52) and dietary fibre (3.9 g per 100 g) from whole wheat atta
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tawa roti made from whole wheat atta has a low glycemic index of 52, producing a moderate glycemic load per serving (~19); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one or two pieces per meal and pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and whole grain fibre supporting lipid management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain tawa roti is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with whole wheat fibre that may support LDL cholesterol reduction; it is a heart-friendly staple when consumed without high-fat accompaniments such as ghee or butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tawa roti is a traditional Indian unleavened flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a pinch of salt, cooked directly on a flat iron griddle called a tawa over medium-high heat. The dough is kneaded until smooth, divided into small balls, rolled thin into rounds approximately 15–20 cm in diameter, and dry-roasted on the tawa until brown spots appear on both sides. Unlike paratha, tawa roti is cooked without added fat, making it one of the leanest Indian bread options. The texture is soft and slightly chewy with a mild, nutty whole wheat flavour. It is a dietary staple across the Indian subcontinent and is widely consumed in Indian communities in Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader South Asian diaspora. Nutritionally, tawa roti provides approximately 297 kcal per 100 g, with 9 g protein, 1.7 g fat, and 61 g carbohydrate, along with 3.9 g dietary fibre from the whole wheat atta. Its low glycemic index of 52 makes it a preferred carbohydrate source for individuals managing blood glucose. It is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free, though it contains gluten. In clinical nutrition, tawa roti is often recommended over white rice or refined-flour breads for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk due to its fibre content and lower glycemic response.

Is tawa roti healthy for diabetics?
Difference between tawa roti and chapati

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Tawa roti (plain chapati without fat) is an exact match in the ICMR-NIN database. GI value of 52 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index, representing a direct analogue for whole wheat chapati cooked on a griddle.
notes: Nutrient values are for plain tawa roti (chapati without added fat) per ICMR-NIN. Restaurant versions served at Dugout - Circular Road (field survey data) may include ghee or butter as a finishing fat, which would increase energy, fat, and saturated fat values. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plain whole wheat flatbread with no hydrogenated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant-based product with no animal fat). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; no significant vitamin C in cooked whole wheat flatbread). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; no vitamin D in plain wheat flatbread). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for plain chapati. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for plain chapati. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for plain chapati. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for plain chapati. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for plain chapati. GI category is low (GI 52 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 61.0 × 0.60) / 100 = 19.1 for a 60 g serving. Multilingual names follow Singaporean Indian cuisine rules: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are all provided. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes use closest_parent match type as no exact tawa roti code exists in either ontology.
